ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pickled cabbage fish noodle soup; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Noodle soup) is the closest available category; no specific code exists for golden soup pickled cabbage fish noodle soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food-dish codes at this level.

main_ingredients: golden broth (turmeric or egg yolk based)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of golden broth with noodles, fish slices, and pickled cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: Fish noodle soup (composite dish) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: wheat noodles (cooked), fish (basa/catfish, cooked), pickled mustard greens, broth
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for golden soup pickled cabbage fish noodle soup. Values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 45% cooked wheat noodles (USDA FDC #20121), 20% cooked fish fillet (basa/catfish, USDA FDC #175177), 5% pickled mustard greens (USDA FDC #11941 adapted), 30% golden broth (estimated from chicken/vegetable broth with turmeric, USDA FDC #171534). Sodium is elevated due to pickled cabbage and seasoning. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles (boiled) — analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for golden soup pickled cabbage fish noodle soup. GI estimated at 52 based on boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The fish, broth, and pickled cabbage components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 45.1) / 100 ≈ 23.5, rounded to 24.2 accounting for 550 g serving with 8.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pickled cabbage and seasoning (~480 mg per 100 g; ~2640 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium content from pickled mustard cabbage and seasoning broth, which can significantly exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles; portion size is the primary driver of postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in reduced portions, as the wheat noodles contribute a moderate glycemic load (GI ~52); requesting a smaller noodle portion or substituting with lower-GI noodles can help manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat; fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids partially offsetting cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease, though the lean fish component provides beneficial protein and omega-3 fatty acids; portion control and requesting reduced-sodium preparation are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Soup Pickled Cabbage Fish Noodle Soup (金汤酸菜鱼面, Jīn tāng suān cài yú miàn) is a Chinese noodle soup dish originating from Sichuan and Chongqing culinary traditions, widely popular across China and served in Chinese restaurants in Singapore. The dish features tender slices of white fish — typically grass carp (草鱼) or basa — poached in a rich, golden-coloured broth made with turmeric, egg yolk, or a combination of yellow chilli and fermented ingredients that give the soup its characteristic golden hue. The broth is layered with tangy, salty pickled mustard cabbage (酸菜, suān cài), which provides a sharp fermented sourness that balances the richness of the soup. Wheat noodles are served submerged in the broth, absorbing the complex flavours of the stock. The dish is finished with aromatics including garlic, ginger, and white pepper, and may include dried chilli for mild heat. Texturally, the fish is silky and delicate, the noodles are soft and chewy, and the pickled cabbage adds a crunchy contrast. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from fish (~5.8 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from noodles (~8.2 g per 100 g), and is relatively low in fat (~2.1 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g, approximately 2640 mg per full bowl) due to the pickled cabbage and seasoned broth, making it a high-sodium dish requiring caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category) based on wheat noodles, making it moderately suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled. The dish is dairy-free and halal-compatible when prepared with permissible ingredients.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA) contains a direct entry for golden soup pickled cabbage fish noodle soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles, cooked fish fillet, pickled mustard greens, and broth. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled wheat noodles) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central; no direct approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or an HPB-equivalent composite dish entry if one becomes available. 2. Sodium is particularly high due to pickled mustard cabbage (suan cai) and seasoned broth; the per-100g value of 480 mg translates to approximately 2640 mg per 550 g bowl, which exceeds the WHO daily recommended limit of 2000 mg in a single serving. 3. GI is estimated at 52 based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI literature. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence across the component sources used. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as the dish uses no hydrogenated oils by standard preparation. 8. The halal flag is set to true based on standard preparation with fish and vegetables; individual restaurant preparation may vary. 9. The restaurant serving this dish (Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles, Lazada One) is noted from the field survey source record; nutrient values are not derived from this source.
